summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends the book 'Designing Interactions' by Bill Moggridge, co-founder of the influential design firm IDEO. The post celebrates the book's comprehensive coverage of digital technology design history, featuring interviews with key industry figures and stunning visuals. Atwood provides context on Moggridge's credentials, including designing the first laptop computer in 1979. He shares his collection of IDEO resources, including their Method Cards and other books, positioning IDEO as a giant in product design. The core message emphasizes that technology should be framed strictly in service to people.
key_points: 'Designing Interactions' is a comprehensive, visually stunning book covering the history of digital technology design with interviews from key figures
Bill Moggridge co-founded IDEO and designed the GRiD Compass, considered the world's first laptop computer, in 1979
IDEO's influence is pervasive - most people have used products they've designed
The IDEO Method Cards serve as prompts to consider different ways design should influence work
The fundamental principle worth remembering: technology should serve people, not the other way around
money_quotes: I think IDEO and Moggridge are on the right track: strictly framing technology in service to people.
I know it seems obvious, but it's one of those things that bears reminding every so often.
They're great little nudges to get you thinking about all the different ways design should influence what you're working on.
Nobody could afford it, of course, but it was an evolutionary milestone in computer history.
Perhaps that's why reading Designing Interactions is so inspiring.
key_insight: Technology exists to serve people, and understanding the history and principles of human-centered design is essential for anyone building digital products.
